blocks.text: Use Case Diagram, High-Level Use Cases, and Expanded Use Cases

This document presents the use case analysis for the University Timetable Optimization System (UTOS) following the analysis-modeling format taught in lecture: a use case diagram showing the system’s interactions with external actors, a high-level use case description for every identified use case, and a detailed (fully dressed) expanded use case specification for every identified use case.

UTOS is a web-based platform for university class timetabling, exam timetabling, role-based access, versioning, notifications, reporting, and controlled timetable repair. The use cases below cover the full SRS scope and trace back to functional requirements identified in the SRS.
Use cases are defined from actor goals rather than from database tables or UI pages. Each use case is presented twice:
High-level use case — brief and structured (use case name, actors, type, description). Quick start for understanding overall scope.
Expanded use case — fully dressed (purpose, overview, type, cross-reference, typical course of events as Actor Action / System Response, and alternative courses).

Use cases are categorized by business importance as Primary (major common processes such as authentication, generation, publication) or Secondary (rare or supporting processes such as version comparison, export, notifications history).

The System under Discussion (SuD) is the UTOS web application: frontend, backend API, database, scheduling solver orchestration, notifications, versioning, and reporting. Human actors remain outside the boundary. The solver engine is treated as an internal supporting actor invoked by the system rather than as a primary actor on the use case diagram.

rows: Full access to timetable setup, generation, review, repair, publishing, versioning, and reporting.
Validates department data, reviews timetable quality, approves or rejects department change requests, and confirms department-level preferences.
Views personal teaching timetable, submits availability, and requests changes when assigned times or rooms need adjustment.
Views the published section timetable and exam schedule; receives notifications about published or changed schedules.
Maintains room information, monitors room availability, and reviews room utilization and capacity reports.
Manages user accounts, role permissions, backups, security settings, audit log visibility, and system configuration.
Internal optimization component invoked by class generation and exam scheduling. Modeled as a supporting actor in flows but not on the use-case diagram.

blocks.text: The use case diagram below illustrates the interactions between the UTOS system and its external actors. Because the full SRS contains too many goals for one clean diagram, the model is split into two views: a Class Timetabling view and an Exam / System-Wide view. A simplified baseline view is also retained for quick reference.

rows:

Figure 4.2

Figure 4.3

blocks.text: The table below lists every use case identified in the system. Each use case is then specified in detail in Section 6 with both a high-level description and a fully dressed expanded specification.

rows: Timetable Administrator (initiator), Department Coordinator, Facility Manager | Primary
Timetable Administrator (initiator), Department Coordinator, Teacher | Primary
Timetable Administrator (initiator), Department Coordinator, Facility Manager | Primary
Department Coordinator (initiator), Timetable Administrator, Teacher | Primary
Timetable Administrator (initiator), Department Coordinator, Teacher, Student | Primary
Timetable Administrator (initiator), Student, Teacher, Facility Manager | Primary
View Exam Schedule and Invigilation Duties
View System Reports and Audit Logs
Timetable Administrator (initiator), Facility Manager, System Administrator, Department Coordinator | Secondary

blocks.text: For each use case below, two specifications are provided: a high-level description (brief, structured) and an expanded specification (fully dressed with typical course of events and alternative courses).
High-Level Use Case: Authenticate and Login

rows: A user opens UTOS and selects a role. The system validates credentials, creates a session, and opens the role-specific dashboard with permitted navigation.

blocks.text: Expanded Use Case: Authenticate and Login

rows: Allow authorized users to enter UTOS with role-appropriate access.
A user selects a role and signs in. The system validates credentials, stores a session, opens the correct dashboard, and enforces role-based navigation. Logout terminates the session.
FR-00.1 to FR-00.6c; NFR-05 (security); role-based access requirements.
1. This use case begins when a user requests access to UTOS.
2. The system asks for identity and role information.
3. The user provides valid login information.
4. The system verifies the user and determines the assigned role.
5. The user confirms the login action.
6. The system starts a session and grants access to permitted functions.
7. The user may later request logout.
8. The system ends the session and returns to the login screen.
Invalid credentials. The system rejects the login and shows a plain-language error.
User has no assigned role. The system denies access and asks the user to contact the system administrator.
Inactive non-admin user. The system may auto-logout according to security settings.

blocks.text: High-Level Use Case: Maintain Organization Hierarchy

rows: Administrator defines the University, Faculty, Department, Batch, Section structure and academic terms so every course, teacher, student, and timetable version belongs to the correct unit.

blocks.text: Expanded Use Case: Maintain Organization Hierarchy

rows: Establish and maintain the organizational structure that anchors all scheduling data.

The administrator creates and maintains the hierarchy of University, Faculty, Department, Batch, and Section, plus academic terms and versioned timetables. All other entities (courses, teachers, sections) reference this hierarchy.
FR-01.1 to FR-01.5; organization hierarchy requirements.
1. This use case begins when the administrator opens organization management.
2. The system displays the existing hierarchy tree and academic terms.
3. The administrator creates or edits a unit (faculty, department, batch, section, or term).
4. The system validates uniqueness, parent linkage, and naming rules.
6. The system stores the unit and refreshes the hierarchy tree.
7. The administrator may move or deactivate a unit.
8. The system updates references and warns about dependent records.
Duplicate or invalid name. The system blocks the save and highlights the conflicting field.
Unit has dependent timetable entries. The system blocks deletion and requires reassignment.

blocks.text: High-Level Use Case: Maintain Academic Calendar

rows: Administrator defines semester dates, holidays, working days, and makeup-class windows so the solver does not place classes on blocked days.

blocks.text: Expanded Use Case: Maintain Academic Calendar

rows: Define the time foundation on which class timetables and exam timetables are generated.

The administrator sets the semester start/end, blackout dates, official holidays, working days per week, and makeup-class windows. The calendar is consumed by both class generation and exam scheduling.
1. This use case begins when the administrator opens the academic calendar settings.
2. The system displays the current term, holidays, and blackout dates.
3. The administrator adds or edits dates and working-day rules.
4. The system validates date ranges, overlaps, and weekend/holiday conflicts.
6. The system stores the configuration and applies it to future generation runs.
Overlapping holiday range. The system warns and asks for correction.
End date before start date. The system blocks the save and highlights the field.

blocks.text: High-Level Use Case: Manage Master Data

rows: Timetable Administrator (initiator), Department Coordinator, Facility Manager
Administrator maintains teachers, rooms, courses, sections, timeslots, room types, capacities, and facilities. The validated data feeds class generation and exam scheduling.

blocks.text: Expanded Use Case: Manage Master Data

rows: Timetable Administrator (initiator), Department Coordinator, Facility Manager
Maintain the validated entity data that the solver and reporting modules consume.
The administrator manages teachers, rooms, courses, sections, timeslots, room capacities, room types, and facilities. This use case also prepares shared room data for the exam module.
FR-02.1 to FR-02.6b; FR-15.1 to FR-15.4 (reusable room data).
1. This use case begins when the Timetable Administrator chooses to maintain scheduling data.
2. The system presents the available master-data categories.
3. The administrator creates or edits a record.
4. The system validates required fields, duplicates, capacity, and relationship rules.
6. The system stores the record and makes it available to generation and reporting.
7. A coordinator or facility manager reviews relevant data.
8. The system shows filtered views according to role.
Required field missing. The system blocks saving and highlights the field.
Room capacity smaller than linked section size. The system warns the user.
Record used by an existing timetable entry. Deletion is blocked or requires replacement.

blocks.text: High-Level Use Case: Define Constraints and Preferences

rows: Administrator configures the active hard rules and weighted soft preferences that the solver uses for class generation.

blocks.text: Expanded Use Case: Define Constraints and Preferences

rows: Configure the rulebook that determines timetable feasibility and quality.

Hard constraints must be enforced (no teacher clash, no room clash, capacity, lab rules, holiday exclusion). Soft preferences (morning preference, early ending, energy saving, room proximity, traffic reduction) are enabled, disabled, or weighted according to institutional policy.
1. This use case begins when the administrator decides to configure scheduling rules.
2. The system shows hard constraints and soft preference options.
3. The administrator chooses active rules and priority weights.
4. The system checks whether the configuration is complete and consistent.
6. The system saves it as the active rule configuration for future generation.
8. The system stores unavailable slots as constraint data.
Soft preference weight outside the allowed range. The system requests correction.
Hard rule disabled in an unsafe way. The system requires confirmation or blocks the change.

blocks.text: High-Level Use Case: Generate Class Timetable

rows: Administrator triggers timetable generation. The system validates data, runs the solver, saves a draft version, and reports score, conflicts, and unplaced sessions.

blocks.text: Expanded Use Case: Generate Class Timetable

rows: Produce a draft class timetable for an academic term.

The administrator starts generation. The system validates data, builds a problem model, runs the solver, saves the result as a draft version, and displays status, score, conflicts, and unplaced sessions.
FR-04.1 to FR-04.7c; FR-05.1 to FR-05.5; versioning requirements.
1. This use case begins when the Timetable Administrator requests generation for an academic term.
2. The system identifies the selected term, departments, sections, courses, teachers, rooms, timeslots, calendar, and constraints.
3. The administrator confirms that generation should begin.
4. The system validates that required data exists and that constraints are usable.
5. The administrator waits for the result.
6. The system attempts to assign every class session to a valid room and timeslot.
7. The administrator reviews the generated result.
8. The system stores the result as a draft version and shows feasibility status, conflicts, unplaced sessions, and quality score.
Master data missing. The system lists missing teachers, rooms, timeslots, courses, or sections and stops generation.
No complete timetable feasible. The system saves the partial result and lists unplaced sessions with reasons.
Conflict detected. The system tries alternate room/time assignments before reporting failure.

blocks.text: High-Level Use Case: Review Timetable Quality

rows: Timetable Administrator (initiator), Department Coordinator, Facility Manager
Administrator and reviewers inspect the draft for conflicts, quality score, room utilization, and teacher load before publishing or repair.

blocks.text: Expanded Use Case: Review Timetable Quality

rows: Timetable Administrator (initiator), Department Coordinator, Facility Manager
Evaluate whether the generated draft is feasible, acceptable, and efficient before publication.
The system shows timetable grid, conflicts, quality score, room usage, teacher load, and soft-constraint penalties. Reviewers decide whether to accept, adjust, or regenerate.
1. This use case begins when the administrator opens a generated timetable draft.
2. The system displays the weekly timetable and summary status.
3. The administrator examines conflicts and warnings.
4. The system shows affected entries and explains each issue.
6. The system shows score, room utilization, teacher load, and penalty breakdown.
7. The administrator decides whether the result is acceptable.
8. The system allows moving forward to locking, re-optimization, or publishing.
Conflict unacceptable. The administrator sends the draft to manual adjustment or re-optimization.
Zero hard conflicts but poor soft score. The administrator may tune preferences and regenerate.

blocks.text: UC07 — Manually Adjust and Lock Timetable
High-Level Use Case: Manually Adjust and Lock Timetable

rows: UC07 — Manually Adjust and Lock Timetable
Administrator manually moves or edits draft entries and locks selected entries so future re-optimization preserves them.

blocks.text: Expanded Use Case: Manually Adjust and Lock Timetable

rows: UC07 — Manually Adjust and Lock Timetable
Allow controlled manual editing and preserve important assignments from future solver changes.
The administrator adjusts entries and locks selected timetable assignments. Locked entries become fixed constraints during re-optimization.
1. This use case begins when the administrator chooses an entry that needs adjustment or preservation.
2. The system shows the selected entry and editable assignment choices.
3. The administrator moves an entry or marks it locked.
4. The system validates the change against teacher, room, section, and timeslot constraints.
6. The system saves the change, records audit history, and updates the draft.
7. The administrator runs future repair if needed.
8. The system keeps locked entries unchanged.
Chosen room already occupied. The system rejects the move and displays the colliding entry.
Entry already published. The system requires a new draft or authorized revision workflow.

blocks.text: High-Level Use Case: Submit Change Request

rows: Teacher or coordinator submits a structured request for a teacher, room, or time change. The request is recorded as Pending and reviewers are notified.

blocks.text: Expanded Use Case: Submit Change Request

rows: Provide a structured way to request schedule changes without direct timetable editing.
A teacher or coordinator submits a request with subject, summary, reason, and optional document. The system records it as pending and notifies reviewers.
1. This use case begins when a teacher or coordinator needs a timetable change.
2. The system offers a request submission function.
3. The requester describes the change and reason.
4. The system validates that the request is connected to a timetable item or valid context.
6. The system records the request with status Pending and notifies reviewers.
Attachment larger than the allowed limit. The system asks the user to remove or replace it.
Request summary empty. The system asks for enough details before submission.

blocks.text: UC09 — Approve or Reject Change Request
High-Level Use Case: Approve or Reject Change Request

rows: UC09 — Approve or Reject Change Request
Reviewer opens a pending request, checks conflicts and details, and approves or rejects it. The requester is notified and approved requests can trigger repair.

blocks.text: Expanded Use Case: Approve or Reject Change Request

rows: UC09 — Approve or Reject Change Request
Review a submitted request and decide whether it should affect the timetable.
A reviewer examines details, checks conflicts, approves or rejects the request, optionally comments, and triggers status notification.
1. This use case begins when a reviewer opens a pending change request.
2. The system displays request details, requester, affected course, current schedule, and proposed change.
4. The system provides conflict check and supporting data.
5. The reviewer approves or rejects the request.
6. The system records the decision, updates request status, and notifies the requester.
7. If approved, the reviewer may trigger timetable repair.
8. The system sends the approved change to re-optimization or manual adjustment workflow.
Conflict check fails. The reviewer may reject the request or route it to re-optimization.
Reviewer lacks authority. The system disables decision buttons.
Administrator triggers repair mode after approved changes. The solver respects locked entries and minimizes disruption to the existing draft.
Repair the timetable after approved changes while minimizing disturbance.
The administrator triggers repair mode. The solver respects locked entries and attempts to change the smallest possible set of timetable entries.
FR-07.1 to FR-07.3; version comparison requirements.
1. This use case begins when an approved change requires timetable repair.
2. The system identifies affected courses, teachers, rooms, sections, and locked entries.
4. The system builds a repair problem preserving accepted and locked entries.
5. The administrator waits for the repair result.
6. The system produces an updated draft and disruption summary.
7. The administrator compares old and new drafts.
8. The system highlights moved, added, and unchanged entries.
No valid repair exists. The system reports unplaced sessions and explains blocking constraints.
Disruption too high. The administrator may reject the repair and keep the previous version.
Timetable Administrator (initiator), Department Coordinator, Teacher, Student
Administrator releases an approved draft as the official live schedule. The system marks one published version per term and notifies affected users.
Timetable Administrator (initiator), Department Coordinator, Teacher, Student
Release the approved timetable as the official live schedule for users.
The administrator confirms publication after review. The system marks the final version as published, makes it visible to teachers and students, and warns about overwriting previous public schedules.
FR-08.1 to FR-08.4b; export and notification requirements.
1. This use case begins when the administrator decides the timetable is ready for release.
2. The system checks whether required approvals and conflict status allow publication.
3. The administrator reviews final publication details.
4. The system shows effective date, timetable name, total entries, visibility, and warning.
6. The system marks the version published, hides older public versions, and notifies users.
7. Teachers and students open the system.
8. The system shows the published timetable filtered to their role.
Conflicts remain. The system blocks publication or requires override authority.
Administrator cancels. The timetable remains a draft.

blocks.text: UC12 — View Personal or Section Timetable
High-Level Use Case: View Personal or Section Timetable

rows: UC12 — View Personal or Section Timetable
Teacher views personal teaching load; student views section schedule. Both see the published timetable filtered by role and identity.

blocks.text: Expanded Use Case: View Personal or Section Timetable

rows: UC12 — View Personal or Section Timetable
Provide each user a clear personal view of the published schedule.

After login, the system filters the published timetable to the user's role: teachers see their teaching schedule; students see the schedule of their section. Both can see day, time, course, room, and counterpart (teacher or section).
FR-08.4; FR-09.1 to FR-09.4; role-based access requirements.
1. This use case begins when a teacher or student opens the dashboard.
2. The system identifies the user role and identity.
3. The user requests their timetable view.
4. The system loads the published version and applies the role filter.
6. The system displays the weekly grid with day, time, course, room, and counterpart.
7. The user may export or print.
8. The system generates the requested PDF/Excel output.
No published timetable exists for the term. The system shows a clear empty-state message.
User not assigned to any section/course. The system shows a notice and a contact link.

blocks.text: High-Level Use Case: Export Class Timetable

rows: User exports the timetable to PDF, Excel, or CSV. The output is structured for printing or further processing.

blocks.text: Expanded Use Case: Export Class Timetable

rows: Allow users to obtain the timetable in usable file formats.
The user selects an export format. The system serializes the timetable with day, time, course, teacher, room, and section into PDF (printable A4), Excel, or CSV.
1. This use case begins when the user requests an export.
2. The system shows export options (PDF, Excel, CSV).
3. The user selects a format and scope.
4. The system collects the corresponding timetable data.
6. The system produces the file and offers it for download.
No timetable selected. The system asks the user to choose a version first.
File generation fails. The system shows an error and offers retry.
The system creates in-app notifications for generation completion, publication, lock/unlock, request status, and version creation. Users view a notification list with unread counts.
Keep users informed about timetable events relevant to their role.

When key events occur (generation complete, publish, lock/unlock, request approve/reject, version-create), the system creates an in-app notification routed to the affected users. Users see a notification history and an unread badge.
1. This use case begins when a notifiable event occurs in the system.
2. The system identifies the event type and the affected user list.
4. The system creates one in-app notification per recipient with title, summary, and link.
5. The user opens the notification panel.
6. The system shows unread count and notification history.
8. The system marks it read and navigates to the related screen.
User has notifications disabled for that category. The system records the notification but does not surface it as unread.
Notification list empty. The system shows an empty-state message.

blocks.text: High-Level Use Case: Compare Timetable Versions

rows: Administrator compares two saved timetable versions side-by-side. The system summarizes added, removed, and changed entries.

blocks.text: Expanded Use Case: Compare Timetable Versions

rows: Provide transparency about how a timetable changed across versions.
The administrator selects two versions of the same term. The system computes a diff and displays added, removed, and changed entries with highlighting and totals.
FR-14.1 to FR-14.3; version comparison requirements.
1. This use case begins when the administrator opens version comparison.
2. The system lists available versions for the selected term.
3. The administrator picks two versions to compare.
6. The system shows side-by-side highlights with added, removed, and changed entries plus totals.
Versions belong to different terms. The system blocks the comparison and warns the user.
No differences. The system shows a clear identical-versions message.

blocks.text: High-Level Use Case: Manage Exam Rooms

rows: Administrator and facility manager maintain exam-suitable room data: capacity, type, layout, and availability windows. Class room data is reused where applicable.

blocks.text: Expanded Use Case: Manage Exam Rooms

rows: Ensure the exam scheduler has accurate room capacity and availability data.
Class rooms are reused as a base. The administrator and facility manager flag exam suitability, examination capacity (often less than class capacity), and exam-window availability.
1. This use case begins when the administrator or facility manager opens exam-room management.
2. The system lists existing rooms with class capacity and exam-suitability flags.
3. The user edits exam capacity, suitability, and availability.
4. The system validates capacity ranges and conflict-free availability windows.
6. The system stores the changes and refreshes exam-scheduler data.
Exam capacity exceeds class capacity. The system warns and asks for confirmation.
Maintenance window conflicts with planned exam day. The system flags the conflict.

blocks.text: High-Level Use Case: Manage Student Hierarchy

rows: Administrator maintains the University to Faculty to Department to Batch to Section to Student hierarchy and section-course enrollment data.

blocks.text: Expanded Use Case: Manage Student Hierarchy

rows: Maintain the student-side of the organization tree so exam scheduling can avoid student double-booking.
The administrator views and edits the nested student hierarchy and imports or updates section membership and course enrollment, often via bulk import.
1. This use case begins when the administrator opens student hierarchy management.
2. The system displays the nested tree and bulk import controls.
3. The administrator imports or edits records.
4. The system validates IDs, parent references, and enrollment links.
6. The system stores the changes and updates downstream views.
Bulk import contains invalid rows. The system reports the row numbers and skips them.
Student already enrolled in another conflicting section. The system warns the administrator.

blocks.text: High-Level Use Case: Manage Exam Courses

rows: Administrator associates courses with exams, defines exam durations, and identifies the sections that will sit each exam.

blocks.text: Expanded Use Case: Manage Exam Courses

rows: Provide the exam-course catalog the scheduler uses to place exam sessions.
The administrator marks which courses have exams, defines exam durations, attaches taking sections, and reviews exam student counts so room capacity can be matched.
1. This use case begins when the administrator opens exam-course management.
2. The system lists courses and their current exam configuration.
3. The administrator marks courses, sets durations, and links sections.
4. The system validates duration limits and section overlaps.
6. The system stores the data and recalculates exam student counts per course.
Duration outside allowed window (e.g., longer than the longest exam slot). The system blocks the save.
Section appears in two exam-courses scheduled at the same time. The system flags the future conflict.
Timetable Administrator (initiator), Student, Teacher, Facility Manager
Administrator generates exam suggestions; the system avoids student and room double-booking. Administrator confirms or modifies and assigns invigilation duties.
Timetable Administrator (initiator), Student, Teacher, Facility Manager
Place exam sessions into rooms and time slots while avoiding student and room clashes.
The administrator configures exam courses, durations, rooms, and student sections. The system suggests room/time placements and invigilation assignments for confirmation.
1. This use case begins when the administrator starts exam scheduling for a term.
2. The system loads exam courses, enrolled sections, students, available rooms, and exam rules.
4. The system finds room/time candidates that avoid room double-booking and student exam overlap.
5. The administrator confirms or modifies suggestions.
6. The system saves exam sessions and updates exam schedule views.
7. Students, teachers, and facility managers request exam views.
8. The system shows schedules relevant to each role.
Exam days running out. The system may allow a maximum of two exams per day per student per the rules.
No room has enough capacity. The system reports the gap and suggests splitting or larger rooms.

blocks.text: UC20 — View Exam Schedule and Invigilation Duties
High-Level Use Case: View Exam Schedule and Invigilation Duties

rows: UC20 — View Exam Schedule and Invigilation Duties
Students view their own exams; teachers view invigilation duties; facility manager checks exam room utilization.

blocks.text: Expanded Use Case: View Exam Schedule and Invigilation Duties

rows: UC20 — View Exam Schedule and Invigilation Duties
Give each role a filtered, role-appropriate view of the exam timetable.
The system filters published exam data by user role: student sees personal exam dates, teacher sees invigilation duties, facility manager sees room-level usage.
1. This use case begins when a student, teacher, or facility manager opens the exam schedule view.
2. The system identifies the role and applies the role filter.
4. The system displays exams (date, time, course, room) or invigilation duties or room utilization.
5. The user may export or print.
6. The system generates the requested file.
No exams scheduled yet. The system shows an empty-state message.
User has no invigilation duties. The system shows a clear notice.

blocks.text: High-Level Use Case: Manage Users and Roles

rows: System administrator creates accounts, assigns roles, performs bulk import, and enforces access permissions across the application.

blocks.text: Expanded Use Case: Manage Users and Roles

rows: Maintain user identities and the role-based access matrix that controls UTOS visibility.

The system administrator creates user accounts (admin, coordinator, teacher, student, facility manager), assigns roles, performs bulk imports, resets passwords, and audits permissions. Role-specific sidebars and screens follow from these assignments.
FR-20.1 to FR-20.5; NFR-05 (security); role-based access requirements.
1. This use case begins when the system administrator opens user management.
2. The system displays the user list, role filters, and bulk-action controls.
3. The administrator creates, edits, or imports user accounts.
4. The system validates email, role, department, and uniqueness.
5. The administrator assigns or revokes roles.
6. The system updates the access matrix and the user's effective sidebar.
7. The administrator may force a password reset.
8. The system sends a reset link or temporary password.
Email already in use. The system blocks creation and highlights the field.
Bulk import contains invalid rows. The system imports valid ones and reports the rest.
Attempt to remove the last admin role. The system blocks the change to prevent lockout.

blocks.text: UC22 — View System Reports and Audit Logs
High-Level Use Case: View System Reports and Audit Logs

rows: UC22 — View System Reports and Audit Logs
Timetable Administrator (initiator), Facility Manager, System Administrator, Department Coordinator
Authorized users view room utilization, teacher load, conflict summaries, quality scores, energy usage, and audit logs (action, timestamp, user, old/new values).

blocks.text: Expanded Use Case: View System Reports and Audit Logs

rows: UC22 — View System Reports and Audit Logs
Timetable Administrator (initiator), Facility Manager, System Administrator, Department Coordinator
Support operational review, accountability, and future timetable improvement.
Users with permission view room utilization, teacher load, conflict summaries, section load, quality score, energy usage, and audit logs.
1. This use case begins when an authorized user requests a report.
2. The system displays report types and filters.
3. The user chooses report type, period, building, department, or term.
4. The system collects relevant timetable, room, teacher, and audit data.
6. The system shows charts, tables, warnings, and export/print options.
8. The system shows action, timestamp, user, and old/new values.
User lacks permission. The report is hidden or access is denied.
No data for the selected period. The system shows an empty-state message rather than failing.

blocks.text: This document captures the use case model for UTOS in the format prescribed by the analysis-modeling lecture: a system-boundary use case diagram and, for each of the 23 identified use cases, both a high-level description and a fully dressed expanded specification with typical course of events and alternative courses.

Primary use cases (authentication, master data, generation, review, manual adjustment, change request handling, re-optimization, publication, exam scheduling, user management) cover the major common processes of the system. Secondary use cases (export, notifications, version comparison, exam viewing, reports) cover supporting and less frequent activities.

Together with the SRS, the architecture diagrams, and the UI evidence, the use case model provides a complete actor-goal view of UTOS that can be traced from requirement to interface and used as the basis for design and testing.
